answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any difficulty breathing, coughing, or chest pain along with your back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and if so, what and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol but it really didn't help much
answers.intake_q8: Any history of cancer, osteoporosis, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or tenderness over the area of your back pain?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any changes in your bowel or bladder function since the pain started?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started approximately three weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, difficulty breathing, coughing, chest pain, numbness, tingling, weakness, or changes in bowel or bladder function. She reports tenderness over the affected area and has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of cancer, osteoporosis, or recent infections. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is mechanical musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage, likely due to soft tissue strain or irritation given the absence of red flag symptoms and the presence of localized tenderness. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain 
2. Intercostal muscle strain or rib dysfunction 
3. Costochondritis or rib inflammation 
4. Early presentation of a visceral cause such as pleuritis (less likely given no respiratory symptoms) 

P: 
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain; max 3 grams/day (adjust for liver disease).
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain and inflammation relief. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with renal impairment or gastrointestinal risk; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs if any new contraindications develop. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek immediate care if she develops new symptoms such as fever, unexplained weight loss, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), changes in bowel or bladder function, or chest pain and difficulty breathing. 
- Testing: No imaging or laboratory testing indicated at this time given the absence of red flag symptoms. Reassess if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks or worsen.

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: Back Injuries | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: extending from your neck to your pelvis. Back injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
